data.title: SA Roundtable: What's next for the rare earths sector?
data.description:
Once obscure, the rare earths sector has been increasingly in the news as the U.S. government moves to diversify sourcing of the elements, which are used in a wide variety of devices, away from China.

In a recent Seeking Alpha poll, we asked readers when they thought the U.S. would become less reliant on China for rare earths. Of the 989 readers who participated, 39% answered 5-10 years, 28.9% said 10-20 years, 16.2% answered 20+ years, and 15.9% said within 5 years.

As part of an effort to diversify sourcing, the U.S. government recently announced it was investing $400 million in American rare earths miner MP Materials (NYSE:MP). The Trump administration has also expressed interest in securing rare earth mining rights in areas such as Greenland.

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Mountain Valley Value Investments, The Methodical Investor, Andrew Hecht and The Global Investor how long they think it will take for the U.S. to become less reliant on China for rare earths, which companies could see investments by the U.S. government, and which countries could become U.S. suppliers.

How long will it take for the U.S. to no longer be dependent on China for rare earths?

Mountain Valley Value Investments: The U.S. is currently heavily reliant on China for rare earth elements, with China controlling about 70% of global production and even more in processing. With the recent Chinese export controls, the U.S. has realized that it can’t rely on a country that is increasingly seen as a geopolitical rival.

With a decades-long reliance, it will take time to reshore the full supply chain. There is a foundation in the MP Materials’ (NYSE:MP) Mountain Pass Mine, the only active rare earth mine in the U.S., but the downstream processing will take years to scale, though it is aided by the recent $400 million Department of Defense investment. Alongside this, companies like USA Rare Earths (USAR) and Rare Element Resources (OTCQB:REEMF) are aiming to open new rare earth mines and processing facilities.

With the government’s support, I expect partial independence by 2030, particularly in lighter rare earths like neodymium and praseodymium. However, full independence, covering both light and heavy rare earth metals (e.g., dysprosium, terbium), will take even longer, if ever.

The Methodical Investor: On average, it currently takes 29 years for a mine to go from discovery to first commercial production in the U.S., the second slowest jurisdiction in the world. Some lawmakers are trying to speed up the process, but complete self-sufficiency is highly unlikely.

However, the good news is that ex-China production is growing in a number of friendly jurisdictions. Lynas Rare Earths (OTCPK:LYSCF) has been growing NdPr production at its Mount Weld mine in Australia. That concentrate is sent to Malaysia, a country that now also mines rare earths for processing.  

It may take years for the U.S. to kick its China dependence, but I doubt it will take decades. These things tend to turn faster than many expect.

Andrew Hecht: Given the speed of the current U.S. administration and the importance of rare earth minerals for technology, military, and other applications, I believe we will see the U.S. seek to become self-sufficient either through domestic mining projects, selective strategic foreign acquisitions, or joint ventures by late 2028.

The Global Investor: This is a very difficult question to answer. However, as The Economist recently noted, BMW and Renault already sell electric vehicles that don't use rare earths in their motors. So, there are signs that industry can move and is already moving away from rare earths. In addition, rare earths are found in several mining jurisdictions, and the bottleneck is more on the processing side. 

The U.S. government and carmakers such as Ford (F), Tesla (TSLA), and Stellantis (STLA) are very active in diversifying supply away from China. For instance, the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation has awarded British company Pensana (OTCPK:PNSPF) a multi-million dollar grant for rare earth mining activities in Angola.

The U.S. government recently made a strategic investment in MP Materials (NYSE:MP). Do you see it investing in any other rare earth companies?

Mountain Valley Value Investments: I think further equity investments like that of MP Materials (MP) will remain rare. Instead, I see the U.S. continuing to support projects through the Defense Production Act, giving financing, price guarantees, and stockpiling programs.

We have already seen this approach with funding to Ucore Rare Metal’s (OTCQX:UURAF) refining facility and Lynas’s (OTCPK:LYSCF) U.S. heavy rare earths separation plant. I can certainly see another wave of support being granted, but not in an equity investment like that of MP Materials.

The Methodical Investor: The MP Materials (MP) deal was interesting in that it saw the DOD take a direct equity stake in the company through its purchase of convertible preferreds and warrants. However, I doubt we’ll be seeing very many such deals in the future for two reasons.

First, MP is a U.S. company with domestic mining operations. But in cases where U.S. companies operate mines in foreign jurisdictions, there may be significant pushback by foreign governments who take a dim view of direct U.S. government ownership of their mineral resources.

Second, MP is a producer. There are other U.S.-based rare earth mining companies, such as USA Rare Earth (USAR), which is developing an NdFeB magnet processing facility as well as the Round Top Mountain REE mine in Texas. But these projects are early stage, and the government may be hesitant to take an ownership stake in unproven, early-stage, pre-production projects.

I can see the government favoring debt financing at preferential rates for most companies while reserving direct equity stakes exclusively for U.S.-based producers with domestic operations.

Andrew Hecht: The U.S. DoD's investment in MP is wrought with issues, as the processing plant is in California, creating significant regulatory and environmental roadblocks. The United States is ranked seventh in rare earth mineral reserves behind, in order of reserves, China, Brazil, India, Australia, Russia, and Vietnam. While tariffs and trade sanctions present roadblocks, strategic investments and partnerships from foreign sources are critical for U.S. acquisitions to meet its future requirements.

The Global Investor: Yes, it’s likely. Consulting group Deloitte, for example, has recommended that the U.S. expand the authority and scope of the Development Finance Corporation to accelerate investment in critical mineral projects abroad.

While the U.S. might face strong competition in Africa from China, there is plenty of rare earth mining in Australia, which is already an important U.S. defense ally.

Do you think the U.S. will try to buy mining rights in Greenland?

Mountain Valley Value Investments: Greenland has vast rare earth potential, but it’s a lot harder than simply buying mining rights. Mining projects require local approval, which cannot be guaranteed.

We’ve already seen companies like Energy Transition Minerals (OTCPK:GDLNF) try to develop the Kvanefjeld rare earth project in the region. This mine would have been capable of producing around 15% of global rare earth metal demand, but the Greenland government banned uranium mining, a by-product of this mine, effectively ending the project. ETM is now stuck in a multi-billion-dollar arbitration.

There is potential though, with the Export-Import Bank's potential financing for Critical Metals (CRML) on the Tanbreez project, a fully permitted project. Any further projects would still take years to develop and get support, so it seems unlikely the U.S. will buy mining rights. The U.S. is better focusing on developing projects in its own borders rather than trying to build mines in one of the hardest regions of the world to mine in. 

The Methodical Investor: I don’t believe efforts to gain control over Greenland are primarily motivated by rare earths. Rather, geopolitical issues, such as an opportunity for greater U.S. military power projection over the North Atlantic and Arctic, are at the heart of the matter. As such, I view the probability of direct U.S. government purchases of mining rights as being low.

However, that doesn’t mean other types of public-private financing arrangements are off the table. An interesting example is the $120 million loan made by the U.S. Export-Import Bank to Critical Metals (CRML) announced in June. It will cover nearly half the cost of its Tanbreez project, located in Greenland, which promises to become a key source of dysprosium, terbium, and yttrium. These types of arrangements are often made at favorable terms for existing equity holders and can give the stock solid momentum.  

Andrew Hecht: Greenland has significant rare earth mineral reserves, but the harsh climate presents mining challenges. Production costs will be sky high, and partnerships will depend on the current administration's relations and approach with Greenland and Denmark.

The Global Investor: It wouldn't surprise me at all. Trump has been talking about this a lot, and it follows a similar playbook to the mining deal struck with Ukraine earlier this year. Given that Greenland is part of Denmark, which is a member of the European Union, and the U.S. has demonstrated significant power over the EU in recent months and still has leverage around trade and defense issues, I can’t see Denmark putting up much of a fight.

Where else could the U.S. procure rare earths?

Mountain Valley Value Investments: Beyond China, the U.S. can tap Australia, with Lynas Rare Earths (OTCPK:LYSCF) supplying from its Mount Weld mine and processing in Malaysia. This already accounts for about 10-20% of non-Chinese output, and they are currently expanding their U.S. facilities.

Another place to look is not from fresh supply but from recycling. One company I find interesting in this space is Mkango (OTCPK:MKNGF). The company holds a 79.4% stake in Maginito, which owns HyProMag, a firm currently doing rare earth magnet recycling in the U.K. What makes them particularly interesting is their HyProMag USA rare earth recycling joint venture with CoTec Holdings (OTCQB:CTHCF). This focuses on breaking down e-waste containing rare earths and transforming them into NdFeB powder at a fraction of the cost of virgin production, with U.S. production set to begin next year.

The Methodical Investor: As the use and profile of rare earths have grown, so has the number of producing countries. There’s a lot of drilling being done in Africa by exploration-stage companies, but they tend to be high-risk microcap plays. Overall, however, the go-to destination that’s quickly emerging for U.S. buyers is Australia.

The Land Down Under is interesting because of its focus on both upstream mining as well as midstream refining. Iluka Resources (OTCPK:ILKAF) is a profitable mineral sands processor that outputs rutile, a titanium dioxide feedstock. But with the help of over $1.6 billion in Aussie government loans, it’s building Australia’s first rare earths refinery. Commissioning is slated for 2027, and the company already has 1 million tonnes of stockpiled concentrate. This is one to keep an eye on as production draws closer. 

 Andrew Hecht: China's strategy has been a model as it controls 70% of rare earth production and a staggering 90% of processing. China has achieved dominance through its domestic production, low environmental regulations, and strategic partnerships with other countries. The U.S. will need to address the domestic environmental risks to ramp up domestic processing and the tariff issues to secure foreign partners in regions rich in rare earth mineral reserves. 

Some of the leading U.S. companies in the rare earth sector are MP (MP), Critical Metals (CRML), USA Rare Earth (USAR), and American Rare Earths (OTCQX:ARRNF). The VanEck Rare Earth and Strategic Metals ETF (REMX) has some exposure, but it is mostly exposed to lithium rather than rare earth minerals.

The Global Investor:  Clearly the U.S. is going to have to get rare earths from friendly countries in the Americas and Africa, as well as Australia. Here are a few stocks to look at in those regions:

In Australia, Iluka Resources Limited (OTCPK:ILKAF) has entered into an agreement with Lindian Resources (OTCQB:LINIF) for the long-term supply of rare earth concentrate, which will support development of Iluka’s Eneabba rare earths refinery in Western Australia, according to the mining publication The Assay. 

Another notable Australian rare earth player is Lynas Rare Earths (OTCPK:LYSCF), which The Wall Street Journal last year called one of the biggest rare earth miners outside of China. Junior Australian companies include Peak Rare Earths (OTCPK:PKREF), which is involved in rare earth elements exploration and development in Australia and Tanzania, and Terrain Minerals (OTCPK:TMXAF), which is involved in rare earth elements exploration in Australia.

In Africa, as well as the aforementioned Pensana, there is Altona Rare Earths, a London-listed company focused on exploration in Botswana and Mozambique. The aforementioned Lindian Resources, an Australia-listed development company, also has rare earth assets in Malawi.

In the Americas, London-listed Rainbow Rare Earths (OTCPK:RBWRF) has development assets in Brazil (and South Africa), while Toronto-listed Aclara Resources (ARA:CA) has projects in Brazil and Chile as well as a rare earths separation facility in the U.S. through its wholly owned subsidiary, Aclara Technologies. In Canada, Toronto-listed Beyond Lithium (OTCQB:BYDMF) has three assets in a Canadian-focused critical minerals exploration portfolio: the Ear Falls Project, the Rare One Project, and the Owl Creek Project.

  • Top Diversified Metals and Mining Stocks

data.title: Short interest across S&P 500 real estate stocks decrease in July
data.description:
At July end, average short interest across S&P 500 real estate stocks decreased to 2.60% of the shares float from 2.65% at the end of June.

Short interest in a stock represents the number of shares sold short but not yet repurchased by the short seller. The metric is often expressed as a percentage of the shares outstanding.

Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) lost 0.70% in July, against the broader S&P 500 index's 2.28% gain.

The estimated weight of XLRE components in the S&P 500 index stood at 1.95% as of Friday, August 15.

Industry analysis

Hotel and Resort REITs stood out as the most shorted subsector in July. Average short interest across the property sector rose to 6.14% from 5.87% in June.

Office REITs took the second spot, with bets against the property sector falling to 5.99% from 6.56%.

Telecom Tower REITs emerged as the least shorted subsector. Short sellers lowered their bets against the property sector to 1.20% from 1.41%.

VICI Properties (VICI) saw a steep fall in short positions to 2.73% from 3.85%. Meanwhile, Weyerhaeuser (WY) logged a notable jump to 2.04% from 1.51%.

Most shorted stocks

  • Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) - 6.06% vs. 5.87% last month
  • BXP (BXP) - 5.98% vs. 6.56% last month
  • Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) - 3.86% vs. 4.40% last month
  • CoStar Group (CSGP) - 3.79% vs. 3.90% last month
  • Realty Income (O) - 3.79% vs. 3.35% last month

Least shorted stocks

  • Crown Castle (CCI) - 1.08% vs. 1.33% last month
  • American Tower (AMT) - 1.17% vs. 1.39% last month
  • Prologis (PLD) - 1.21% vs. 1.16% last month
  • CBRE Group (CBRE) - 1.35% vs. 1.34% last month
  • Simon Property Group (SPG) - 1.41% vs. 1.43% last month

data.title: Airbus, Boeing rivalry to reach a key milestone next month
data.description:
Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) is set to break a coveted title Boeing (NYSE:BA) has held for decades, as its A320 series is just weeks away from overtaking its U.S. competitor’s 737 family as the most-delivered commercial airliner in history, according to data from aviation consultancy Cirium.

As of early August 2025, the European aircraft maker has delivered 12,153 737s since Air France (OTCPK:AFRAF) (OTCPK:AFLYY) took delivery of the first 100 units of its single-aisle jetliner in March 1988, Cirium data showed.

Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) 737 deliveries had approached 12,175 after the company handed the first 100 of its cigar-shaped bestseller to Lufthansa (OTCQX:DLAKF) (OTCQX:DLAKY) in December 1967.

Max Kingsley-Jones, Head of Advisory at Cirium, said that “current delivery rates should see the two paths cross very soon,” and added that within the next month or so, cumulative deliveries of A320s could overtake those of 737.

Boeing (NYSE:BA) had already delivered about 1,500 737s by 1988 when Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) began manufacturing its answer to the U.S.-made narrow-body aircraft.

“But did anyone back then expect it could become number one—and on such high production volumes?” Kingsley-Jones asked. “I certainly didn’t, and nor probably did Airbus Industrie...”

data.title: Zelenskyy to meet Trump on Monday after U.S.-Russia talks in Alaska end in no deal
data.description:
Ukraine's leader, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, on Saturday said he would see U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday and that he and European leaders supported a trilateral meeting between him, Trump, and Russian President Vladimir Putin.

Zelenskyy's comments come after Trump and Putin gathered in Alaska on Friday, a meeting that Trump described as "very productive," even though no agreement was reached to resolve a war that has raged for nearly three and a half years.  

"Ukraine reaffirms its readiness to work with maximum effort to achieve peace. President Trump informed about his meeting with the Russian leader and the main points of their discussion. It is important that America’s strength has an impact on the development of the situation," Zelenskyy said on X (formerly Twitter). 

"We support President Trump’s proposal for a trilateral meeting between Ukraine, the USA, and Russia. Ukraine emphasizes that key issues can be discussed at the level of leaders, and a trilateral format is suitable for this," Ukraine's President said.

"On Monday, I will meet with President Trump in Washington, D.C., to discuss all of the details regarding ending the killing and the war. I am grateful for the invitation," he added. 

Trump in the lead-up to Alaska had downplayed expectations on reaching a deal to end the war and stressed that the more important meeting would be the one between him, Putin, and Zelenskyy.

"As President Trump said ‘there's no deal until there's a deal'. As envisioned by President Trump, the next step must now be further talks including President Zelenskyy, whom he will meet soon. We are also ready to work with President Trump and President Zelenskyy towards a trilateral summit with European support," European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, French President Emmanuel Macron, Italy's Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, UK's Prime Minister Keir Starmer, Finnish President Alexander Stubb, Poland's Prime Minister Donald Tusk, and European Council President António Costa said in a joint statement on Friday. 

Media reports earlier this month said that a possible deal to end the war could involve swapping of territories or of Russia locking in territories already captured. Zelenskyy has rejected any such notions.

"We are clear that Ukraine must have ironclad security guarantees to effectively defend its sovereignty and territorial integrity," the European leaders said. "It will be up to Ukraine to make decisions on its territory. International borders must not be changed by force," they added.   

Hopes of progress on Russia and Ukraine helped Wall Street post solid gains on Friday. Meanwhile, WTI crude oil futures (CL1:COM) ended the week -0.3% lower.

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU). 

And here are some popular oil-focused ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (NYSEARCA:DBO), (BATS:OILK), and (NYSEARCA:USL). 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Insider trades: Eli Lilly, Boeing, Nike among notable names this week
data.description:
Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) and Nike (NYSE:NKE) were among the several companies that witnessed insider trades this week. Below is a list of purchases and sales made between August 11 and August 15.

  • Eli Lilly’s (NYSE:LLY) CEO David Ricks bought 1,632 shares at $644.77 apiece, totaling about $1.05 million. His ownership at the firm now stands at 676,970 shares.
  • Scott Smith, CEO of Viatris (NASDAQ:VTRS), purchased 22,000 shares at $9.99 each, for a total of about $219,740. His stake now stands at 314,807 shares.
  • Anton Levy, director at Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD), purchased 325,000 shares for about $11.00 per share, worth about $3.55 million. His total ownership is now 949,000 shares.
  • Gregory Hayes, director at Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX), acquired 8,350 shares at $119.90 each, for a transaction value of around $1 million. His holdings have increased to 29,423 shares.
  • Gregg Winiarski, director at Strategy (NASDAQ:MSTR), bought 10,000 shares at $97.65 each, for a total of roughly $976,520. His current stake stands at 10,000 shares.
  • At Boeing (NYSE:BA), David Raymond, the CEO of Boeing Global Services, sold 3,770.77 shares at $229.95 each, for proceeds of about $867,069. He now holds 45,754.09 shares in the company.
  • Nike’s (NYSE:NKE) Chief Innovation, Design & Product Officer Philip McCartney disposed of 3,162 shares at $77.21 apiece, totaling roughly $244,138. His remaining stake in the company is 37,379 shares.
  • Philip Boudreau, EVP and CFO of Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT), sold 5,550 shares at $134.55 each, generating about $746,758. He retains 51,003 shares of ABT.
  • Duke Energy’s (NYSE:DUK) Executive Vice President and Chief General Officer Thomas Gillespie sold 6,700 shares at $124.26, amounting to about $832,542. He now owns 46,825 shares.
  • At United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL), Director Barney Harford sold 24,823 shares in the price range of $90.03 and $97.76 each, with total proceeds of about $1.42 million. His holdings now stand at 91,000 shares.

data.title: Crypto stocks, fintechs post biggest weekly swings: financials roundup
data.description:
Financial stocks largely rose with the broader equity market this week as earnings season cooled down, with the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) advancing 1.2% and the S&P 500 Index gaining 1%.

TeraWulf (WULF) rose the most of any financial stock for the week ended Aug. 15, 2025, surging 78% after Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) revealed it's taking an 8% stake in the crypto miner;

Fellow bitcoin (BTC-USD) miner Hut 8 (HUT) climbed 20% as the price of bitcoin briefly touched new all-time highs during the week;

UNM Holdings (UWMC) accelerated 17%;

Insurance company Lemonade (LMND) gapped up 17%; and

BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR), an ether (ETH-USD) treasury company, rounded out the five biggest winners with the 12% rise.

On the negative side, retail trading platform eToro (ETOR) tumbled 17% even after posting strong Q2 results;

Upstart Holdings (UPST), which during the week priced $600M in convertible debt, dipped 6.8%;

Circle Internet Group (CRCL) slid 6.1% after the stablecoin issuer posted stronger-than-expected Q2 revenue and priced a 10M-share public offering;

Galaxy Digital (GLXY) also retreated 6.1% after the crypto investment firm closed a $1.4B project financing facility to expand its Helios data center; and

Argentine lender Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL) drifted down 5.4%.

data.title: Air Canada suspends operations as flight attendants go on strike
data.description:
Air Canada (OTCQX:ACDVF) and its low-cost affiliate Air Canada Rouge on Saturday suspended all operations after its unionized flight attendants went on strike, impacting nearly 700 flights operated by Canada's largest carrier.

“We are now officially on strike,” the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE), which represents the striking flight attendants, said in a statement as the trade union action took effect at 12:58 am ET.

After CUPE issued a 72-hour strike notice on Aug. 13, Air Canada (OTCQX:ACDVF) has been gradually phasing out its operations ahead of the walkout, which it said could impact about 130,000 of its passengers.

On Saturday, the company, the foreign carrier with the highest number of flights to the U.S., began a lockout action against striking employees at 1:30 am ET.

The flight attendants walked off the jobs after CUPE and Air Canada failed to reach an agreement over wage increases and payments for uncompensated groundwork, including the boarding process.

“At this time, Air Canada remains engaged and committed to negotiating a renewal to its collective agreement with CUPE,” the Montreal-based carrier said, adding it “deeply regrets the effect the strike is having on customers.”

data.title: AI Series: Which countries are leading the AI race?
data.description:
Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates once said artificial intelligence is the most important technological advancement and is as revolutionary as mobile phones and the internet.

The global AI market is projected to soar from $189 billion in 2023 to $4.8 trillion by 2033, according to a report by the United Nations, implying a 25-fold increase in just a decade.

AI is already reshaping markets and altering the balance of power among nations. Naturally, companies and governments across the globe are spending like maniacs to stay ahead. Today’s article focuses on where different countries stand when it comes to AI spending.

Seeking Alpha has started an AI series wherein, each weekend, we will write on trending models and latest announcements, including key insights on valuation and metrics.

The United States

The U.S. by far remains the top investor when it comes to AI. The largest private technology giants, including Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOG) said they intend to spend near $400 billion this year.

According to a report by PitchBook in July, startup funding in the first six months of the year jumped to $162.8B, with AI deal activity accounting for 64% of all deal value and 35% of deal counts. U.S. startup OpenAI alone only accounted for a $40B funding round, while Meta spent $14.3B for a stake in Scale AI.

Mira Murati’s Thinking Machines Lab raised $2B at a valuation of $12B in a funding round, while Amazon (AMZN) and Google-backed (GOOG) Anthropic is reportedly nearing completion of a new funding deal that would generate $3B to $5B and propel the AI startup to a $170B valuation. Other blockbuster AI deals included significant investments in Safe Superintelligence, Anduril, and Anysphere.

The U.S. government reportedly invested about $328 billion in AI between 2019 and 2023.

In 2024, U.S. private AI investment grew to $109.1 billion—nearly 12 times China’s $9.3B and 24 times the U.K.’s $4.5 billion, according to the 2025 AI Index Report from Stanford University.

Top AI models include ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama, and Claude, among others.

 China

Although there is a huge gap in AI spending when it comes to the U.S. and China, the launch of AI startup DeepSeek this year put the world’s second-largest economy on the global stage.

Since 2013, China has made substantial AI investments totaling $119.3B until last year, with companies including Alibaba (BABA), Baidu (BIDU) and Tencent taking charge.

Top AI models from China include Baidu’s Ernie Bot, Moonshot AI’s Kimi, Alibaba's Qwen, and AI startup Zhipu’s newly released open-source model GLM-4.5.

United Kingdom

The UK is the third largest AI market in the world and is valued at $92B in 2024.

Earlier this year, British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said he wanted to make the UK an AI "superpower," adding that the country was already the European leader for AI investment. A study by venture capital firm Accel last year also found that the United Kingdom is home to the highest number of GenAI startups across Europe and Israel.

Other than the famous DeepMind, the UK also boasts of startups like ElevenLabs with a valuation of $3.3B, Synthesia with a $2.1B valuation, and Quantexa with a reported valuation of $2.6 billion.

According to PitchBook, European AI companies raised over $13B in 2024, marking a 22% increase in capital invested, with the U.K. alone securing $6B in AI funding during the year. France is also trying to carve out its space with its champion startup, Mistral AI, which has a valuation of $6B and competes with giants including OpenAI and Google.

A report by Silicon Valley Bank shows that across the U.K., France, and Germany markets, large-scale AI funding rounds are led by U.S. investors like Softbank, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Coatue Management, among others.

Other countries, including Canada, Israel, India, South Korea, and Singapore, have also attracted significant AI investments over the past decade.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Union Pacific, Lumentum and KBR among top picks
data.description:
The S&P500 (SP500) closed in the red on Friday, after a week that saw a significantly hotter than expected July PPI report, more tariff-related headlines, and a strong earnings report from Micron Technology.

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) gained 1.5%, while Dow (DJI) advanced 2%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Argus downgraded Union Pacific over merger concern

Argus lowered its rating on Union Pacific (NYSE:UNP) to Hold from Buy on concerns over its merger with Norfolk Southern (NYSE:NSC) that would combine the two railroads, forming the first transcontinental railroad in the U.S. 

Analyst Kristina Ruggeri said that while the firm ultimately believes that Union Pacific will benefit from the merger, the merged rails giant is seen likely experiencing integration challenges that will negatively impact its profitability in the near term.

"If the merger does not go through, the company could be at a competitive disadvantage without access to East Coast ports. In the meantime, it will also face its own export challenges amid tariffs," highlighted Ruggeri.

Lumentum upgraded after Q4 results

BofA upgraded Lumentum (NASDAQ:LITE) to Neutral from Underperform and raised PT to $135 from $78 after its fourth quarter results and outlook.

Analysts led by Vivek Arya said that Lumentum's results indicate the possibility of hitting both its medium-term ($600M/quarter) and longer-term ($750M/quarter) revenue run-rate two-to-three quarters in advance of BofA's prior model.

"Under its relatively new CEO, LITE is refocusing back to its best-of-breed optical component (high-speed lasers, optical circuit switches) roots while prudently managing its lower margin optical transceiver mix. The result is a faster path towards achieving or beating the target 40% GM [gross margin]," said BofA.

KBR cut to Neutral at BofA

BofA downgraded KBR (NYSE:KBR) to Neutral from Buy, believing the U.S. Department of Defense's cancellation of the $20B HomeSafe contract - which had been expected to be a key medium-term growth driver, combined with other program cancellations and a lack of near-term new catalysts, will give the market pause.

The brokerage also reduced PT to $55 from $70, with analyst Mariana Perez Mora saying that a change in global energy priorities, delayed liquefied natural gas decisions in the U.S., and the lack of insight on near-term catalysts continue to pressure valuation of KBR's stronger Sustainable Technology Solutions portfolio.

Wells Fargo increased the price target on Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) to $220 from $185 ahead of the company's quarterly results later this month. The analysts noted that strong intra-quarter data points and reports of approved licenses to resume H20 chip sales into China, have driven them to increase Q2 estimates above Nvidia's $45.0B +/-2% guidance—they have moved to $48.2B/$1.06 (prior estimate of $45.8B/$1.00). The firm maintained its Overweight rating on the stock.

Meanwhile, Citi maintained its Neutral rating and PT of $180 on Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) following a reported deal to give 15% of revenues from Chinese AI chips sales to the U.S. government in exchange for export licenses.

Goldman Sachs upgraded Clearwater Analytics (NYSE:CWAN) to Buy from Neutral, believing that execution risk tied to the acquisitions is now more than priced in, and the brokerage sees a positive catalyst path for the stock from here.

CIBC upgraded energy infrastructure company Williams (NYSE:WMB) to Outperform from Neutral with a $64 price target, raised from $61, citing increasingly bullish natural gas demand trends and the outlook for infrastructure.

data.title: Cencora directors ink $111M deal related to opioid claims
data.description:
Cencora (NYSE:COR) directors have agreed to pay more than $111M in cash to resolve claims by pension funds that they failed to take adequate measures to prevent the company’s involvement in the U.S. opioid crisis, Bloomberg News reported, citing court documents.

According to a filing in the Delaware Chancery Court on Friday, the deal relates to a case filed by Lebanon County Employees Retirement Fund and a Teamsters health services and insurance plan in 2021.

Following the intervention of a mediator in July, Cencora (NYSE:COR), previously known as AmerisourceBergen, agreed to the deal in exchange for a $111.2M cash payment, the court filings suggest.

The lawsuit had accused Cencora (NYSE:COR) directors of “failing to adopt, implement, or oversee reasonable policies and practices to prevent the unlawful distribution of opioids” and ignoring “evidence of widespread illegal opioid sales.”

Separately in 2021, Cencora (COR) and its rival medical distributors McKesson (MCK) and Cardinal Health (CAH), along with J&J (JNJ), reached a $26B deal with a group of state attorneys general to settle claims that they helped fuel the U.S. opioid epidemic.
